text: Unified Payments Interface (UPI) Guide Introduction Unified Payments Interface (UPI) is an instant real-time payment system developed by the National Payments Corporation of India (NPCI). It allows users to transfer money directly between bank accounts using mobile devices. Key Features  Instant money transfer  24×7 availability  Interoperability across banks  Mobile-first payment experience  Secure authentication using UPI PIN What is a UPI ID? A UPI ID, also called a Virtual Payment Address (VPA), is a unique identifier used to send and receive money. Examples: user@oksbi, user@paytm, user@ybl How to Register for UPI 1. Install a UPI-enabled application. 2. Verify your mobile number. 3. Select your bank account. 4. Create a UPI PIN. 5. Start making transactions. How to Send Money 1. Open your UPI application. 2. Select "Send Money". 3. Enter UPI ID or scan QR code. 4. Enter amount. 5. Confirm using UPI PIN. How to Receive Money 1. Share your UPI ID. 2. Generate a payment request. 3. Receive funds instantly. Transaction Limits Banks may set daily transaction limits. Most UPI applications support transactions up to ■1 lakh per day, subject to bank policies. Security Best Practices  Never share UPI PIN.  Never share OTP.  Verify recipient details before payment.  Avoid unknown payment requests.  Use trusted applications only. Common Frauds Collect Request Scam: Fraudsters send payment requests claiming rewards or refunds. QR Code Scam: Scanning a QR code generally sends money, not receives money. Fake Customer Support: Fraudsters impersonate bank representatives and request sensitive information. Frequently Asked Questions Q: Is UPI free? A: Most person-to-person transactions are free. Q: Can UPI work without a bank account? A: No. Q: Can I have multiple UPI IDs? A: Yes. Q: What should I do if money is debited but not received? A: Contact your bank and raise a dispute.

RBI Financial Fraud Prevention Handbook Overview Digital banking has increased convenience but also created opportunities for cybercriminals. Consumers must remain vigilant. Common Types of Fraud Phishing Fake emails requesting confidential information. Smishing Fraudulent SMS messages containing malicious links. Vishing Fraudulent phone calls impersonating banks. Investment Fraud Promises of unrealistic returns. Loan App Fraud Unauthorized lending applications collecting personal data. QR Code Fraud Scammers trick users into scanning codes that trigger payments. Warning Signs  Guaranteed profits  Urgent payment requests  Requests for OTP  Requests for card PIN  Requests for CVV Banking Safety Tips  Use official banking applications  Enable two-factor authentication  Update passwords regularly  Avoid public Wi■Fi for banking Cyber Hygiene  Keep devices updated  Install antivirus software  Download apps only from official stores  Monitor account activity frequently Reporting Fraud National Cyber Crime Portal: www.cybercrime.gov.in Cyber Helpline: 1930 RBI Recommendations  Verify before you trust  Think before you click  Never share banking credentials Case Study A fraudster impersonates a bank officer and requests OTP verification. The customer shares OTP and loses funds. This demonstrates why OTP must never be shared. Key Takeaways  RBI never asks for passwords  Banks never ask for PINs  Genuine institutions never request OTPs

Understanding Interest Rates and Banking Introduction Interest is the price paid for borrowing money and the reward earned for saving money. Simple Interest Formula: SI = (P × R × T) / 100 Where: P = Principal R = Rate T = Time Compound Interest Formula: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt) Where: A = Final Amount P = Principal r = Interest Rate n = Compounding Frequency t = Time Types of Interest Rates  Fixed Interest Rate  Floating Interest Rate  Effective Annual Rate  Nominal Interest Rate RBI and Monetary Policy The Reserve Bank of India regulates monetary policy through:  Repo Rate  Reverse Repo Rate  Cash Reserve Ratio  Statutory Liquidity Ratio Repo Rate The rate at which RBI lends money to commercial banks. Effect of Interest Rates Higher Interest Rates  Reduce borrowing  Increase savings Lower Interest Rates  Encourage borrowing  Promote spending Savings Products  Savings Accounts  Fixed Deposits  Recurring Deposits Loans  Home Loans  Education Loans  Personal Loans  Vehicle Loans Factors Affecting Loan Rates  Credit Score  Income  Loan Tenure  Economic Conditions Financial Planning Tips  Compare rates before borrowing  Maintain a healthy credit score  Avoid excessive debt

Investor Education and Financial Planning Introduction Investing helps individuals grow wealth and achieve financial goals. Why Invest?  Beat inflation  Create wealth  Achieve financial goals  Generate passive income Asset Classes Equity Ownership in companies. Mutual Funds Professionally managed investment pools. Bonds Debt instruments issued by governments and corporations. Fixed Deposits Low-risk savings instruments. Risk and Return Generally, higher expected returns involve higher risk. Diversification Diversification reduces investment risk by spreading money across multiple asset classes. SIP (Systematic Investment Plan) Invest fixed amounts regularly into mutual funds. Benefits:  Rupee cost averaging  Disciplined investing  Long-term wealth creation Financial Goal Planning Short-Term Goals  Emergency fund  Vacation  Gadget purchase Medium-Term Goals  Vehicle purchase  Higher education Long-Term Goals  Retirement  Home purchase Investor Rights  Right to information  Right to grievance redressal  Right to fair treatment Common Investment Mistakes  Chasing past returns  Lack of diversification  Emotional investing  Ignoring risk profile SEBI Investor Protection Measures  Market regulation  Disclosure requirements  Investor awareness programs  Grievance resolution systems Sample Financial Plan Monthly Income: ■50,000 Allocation:  20% Savings  15% Mutual Funds  10% Emergency Fund  5% Insurance  Remaining for expenses
